title: Reaffirming the deep and steadfast United States-Canada partnership and the ties that bind the two countries in support of economic and national security.
plain_summary: This bill is about the friendship between the United States and Canada. It says that Canada is America's best friend and biggest trading partner. The two countries work together on important things like keeping their borders safe, sharing energy, and stopping dangerous drugs from being sold. The House of Representatives wants to make this friendship even stronger so both countries can create more jobs, stay safer, and protect the same important values like democracy.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution reaffirms the deep and steadfast United States-Canada partnership and the ties that bind the two countries in support of economic and national security.

sections.title: Background and Facts
sections.text: Congress understands how important it is to strengthen United States alliances. The House of Representatives must protect and advance United States partnerships. The United States is fortunate to have one of its closest allies, Canada, as a neighbor. This is especially important when countries around the world face serious threats from their neighbors. In June two thousand twenty three, Congress created a bipartisan group called the American Canadian Economy and Security Caucus. This group works to strengthen the economic and security partnership between the United States and Canada. The United States and Canada can work together in four important areas. These are economic security, energy and critical minerals security, national security, and global security. Americans and Canadians both benefit from their economic relationship and connected supply chains. The United States Mexico Canada Agreement forms the foundation of their economic competitiveness. The United States and Canada share one of the largest trading relationships in the world. In two thousand twenty three, they traded nearly one trillion dollars worth of goods and services. This trade supports nearly eight million American jobs. They understand how important secure supply chains are and have created formal ways to strengthen economic ties and reduce America's dependence on foreign enemies. Canada is the largest single export market for the United States. Canada was the number one customer for thirty six of the fifty states in two thousand twenty three. Over three hundred thirty congressional districts each exported more than two hundred fifty million dollars in goods to Canada. Over one hundred districts each exported more than one billion dollars in goods to Canada in two thousand twenty three. Trade in agricultural products between Canada and the United States reached seventy two and a half billion dollars in two thousand twenty three. Canada is the number one agricultural export market for twenty seven states. Canada United States trade is built on long standing supply chains where roughly seventy percent of Canadian goods exports to the United States are used by American manufacturers to produce higher value goods. Canada buys over twenty two billion dollars worth of automotive parts and thirty three and six tenths billion dollars worth of vehicles from the United States each year. Canadian lumber plays a crucial role in housing affordability for Americans because demand for lumber exceeds what United States domestic mills can supply. The United States and Canada are global leaders in science, technology, and innovation. They can secure North America's future as the most competitive region in the world. They are working together to deepen cooperation in developing and protecting emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence and quantum computing. Canada is the world's fourth largest petroleum producer and is the United States largest foreign supplier of energy, including oil, uranium, natural gas, and electricity. Canada supports American energy dominance by providing safe and reliable natural gas, electricity, crude oil, and uranium for nuclear power. Canada helps the United States position as the world's number one exporter of liquified natural gas by supplying border states with Canadian natural gas. Canada enables United States artificial intelligence technology growth by supplying the critical fuels required by the American power industry. Canada is a reliable source of energy and resources for the United States, producing over sixty minerals and metals. Canada is a leading global producer of twenty one critical minerals on the United States Geological Survey critical mineral list. Canada is committed to ensuring North American competitiveness and the success of Canadian and United States workers and communities. Canada is taking steps to address China's unfair market practices by screening investment coming into Canada and applying extra taxes on Chinese products such as Chinese electric vehicles, steel and aluminum. The United States and Canada have a deeply connected electricity sector with more than thirty five active electricity transmission connections between the two countries. Many of these connections allow electricity to flow in both directions, helping to ensure the security and reliability of the North American grid. They have committed to work together to protect diverse natural areas that span their shared border, including working with Indigenous and Tribal partners. This benefits shared species like migratory birds. They have worked together for over one hundred years under the Boundary Waters Treaty to manage and conserve their shared waters for the benefit of both countries, including over fifty years under the Great Lakes Water Quality Agreement. The United States and Canada share three oceans and the world's longest border. They safely oversee the movement of about four hundred thousand people every day and more than two and a half billion dollars worth of goods and services that cross the border each day. They cooperate to keep the border open to legitimate trade and travel but closed to illegal migration, terrorists, criminals, and threats to citizen health and safety. They are committed to jointly protecting the security of their citizens, including through Canada's recent actions and significant investments to strengthen border security. These actions include fighting sources of illegal migration at the border and keeping deadly drugs like fentanyl and its precursors from entering. They are securing border crossings by maintaining twenty four hour seven day surveillance using new technology and increased personnel. They are combating fentanyl trafficking through the appointment of a Fentanyl Czar, listing cartels as terrorist entities, and launching a Canada United States Joint Strike Force. They are detecting and disrupting the fentanyl trade with more technology, tools and intelligence. They are reinforcing a one border one team approach through more cross border information and intelligence sharing. They are keeping people safe through joint emergency readiness and creating a North American Aerospace Defense Command like Joint Emergency Management partnership. They are united in fighting a fentanyl crisis which is affecting citizens on both sides of the border and is fueled by the actions of harmful actors abroad. They work together to secure the countries' border through various programs including the Cross Border Crime Forum, Integrated Border Enforcement Teams, Beyond the Border Initiative, United States Canada NEXUS Trusted Traveler Program, Border Enforcement Security Taskforces, Shiprider, the Integrated Cross Border Maritime Law Enforcement program, and United States preclearance operations conducted at Canadian airports. All of these enhance joint security efforts. They have an Integrated Border Enforcement Charter that allows border enforcement agencies to jointly identify national security threats, disrupt organized criminal activities, seize drugs and weapons, and intercept criminal networks trying to smuggle people across the border. Both countries understand that a threat to the security of one country is a threat to the security of both. The United States and Canada are Pacific, Atlantic, and Arctic countries. They are completely committed to playing a leadership role in protecting global security and promoting democracy around the world. They recognize that collective security is a shared responsibility and are committed to expanding cooperation on continental defense and in the Arctic. This includes increasing investments in continental defense and modernizing NORAD, the world's only binational military command. They share the desire for a peaceful, stable, and predictable Arctic region, including for the benefit of Arctic and Northern peoples and communities. They work together to advance democratic principles, human rights, and free trade policies through various international organizations including the G7, the G20, the United Nations, the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe, the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development, the World Trade Organization, and the Organization of American States. They cooperate extensively through a Tri Command Framework made up of the United States Northern Command, the Canadian Joint Operations Command, and the North American Aerospace Defense Command. They work together as the only North American members of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization to ensure peace and security in the transatlantic region. They support NATO's deterrence and defense efforts and their European Allies through their roles as the Framework Nations for the NATO brigades in Latvia and Poland. They share a long and storied history of civil space partnership between the National Aeronautics and Space Administration and the Canadian Space Agency. A Canadian will fly on the historic Artemis II mission around the Moon with NASA. Canada has been a committed ally in upholding the rules based international order by promoting peace, resilience, and security in the Indo Pacific through an increased and diversified military presence. Canada has been a reliable and engaged partner of the United States in the Indo Pacific by working extensively with United States Indo Pacific Command. This includes bilateral and multilateral exercises, regional security cooperation and defense engagements, involvement in regional defense forums, and ultimately through unwavering support of a free, open, and inclusive Indo Pacific. Canada is in consultation with the United States, Australia, and the UK to identify collaborative projects on advanced capabilities under AUKUS Pillar II. History, geography, commerce, security, and shared democratic values form the foundation of the close relationship between the United States and Canada.

sections.title: Resolution Statements
sections.text: Therefore, the House of Representatives recognizes that now more than ever, the relationship between the United States and Canada is an essential strategic asset to the United States and Americans. This relationship is critical to promoting peace, expanding global economic opportunity, and being prepared to respond to unforeseen events. The House reaffirms its full commitment to maintain and grow the critical United States Canada partnership. The House recognizes that the security of one country depends on the security of the other and welcomes greater collaboration in the areas of defense, cyber and technology security, and Arctic security. The House reaffirms its commitment to the bilateral and international alliance between the two countries, which allows both countries to face common threats together and uphold common values, including democracy, human rights, and the rule of law. The House recognizes the strategic importance of one of the most secure borders in the world. The joint management of this border facilitates trade and serves as a trusted corridor for their supply chains. The House recognizes that strengthening the supply chains of both countries will make both countries more competitive and more resilient in the face of economic aggression from hostile countries. The House supports an increased focus on energy security through greater cross border energy infrastructure, including for oil, natural gas, nuclear energy, renewable energy, and resilient electricity transmission, and through diversifying critical minerals supply chains. The House is fully committed to the creation of more well paying United States jobs through continued trade and investment with Canada.
